script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed one point nine nine percent down on the session. It was a broad and painful day, with semiconductors carrying the heaviest damage.

Sandisk collapsed twelve point six three percent on Tuesday's session. That kind of single-day drawdown in a single name sends a message to the entire memory sector — and this portfolio felt it across multiple positions simultaneously.

No macro data was available to contextualize Tuesday's broader move, but the pressure was unmistakably sector-specific. Semiconductor and chip-adjacent names led declines across the board, with the Nasdaq closing sharply lower on the session as investors rotated out of chip stocks and into oversold enterprise software names. The rotation was visible in real time.

The deepest story of the session centers on the semiconductor selloff and what it means for this portfolio's chip-heavy concentration. Micron fell four point three two percent. Advanced Micro Devices fell four point two one percent. Intel fell six point one two percent. Nvidia fell three point five two percent. According to reporting from Yahoo Finance, investors appeared to be taking profits in chip stocks while rotating into enterprise software — names like Elastic and SentinelOne surged in the afternoon session as a direct consequence. The backdrop here matters: ASML, the world's largest supplier of chip manufacturing equipment, reports quarterly earnings Wednesday, and investors are already pricing in uncertainty around U.S. export control legislation that could restrict allied nations from selling chip tools to China. That overhang is sitting on the entire sector heading into Wednesday's open.

Now to the holdings rundown.

Nvidia was down three point five two percent on the session. The ASML earnings report Wednesday is the most immediate catalyst to watch — Nvidia's supply chain runs directly through ASML's extreme ultraviolet lithography tools, and any guidance revision on capacity or China exposure will land on Nvidia's valuation. Separately, a prominent AI investor noted in Tuesday's coverage that the real cash flow winners of the AI boom may not be the model builders, flagging Nvidia as a primary beneficiary of infrastructure spending redistribution. Watch ASML closely Wednesday.

Apple was up zero point six three percent on the session — one of the few green prints across the portfolio Tuesday. Two developments deserve attention. IDC reported that China smartphone shipments fell four point three percent in the second quarter, the fifth consecutive quarterly decline, but Apple was one of only two vendors to post growth, with shipments up twenty four point four percent year over year. That is a meaningful outperformance in a deteriorating market. Separately, Apple sued OpenAI last week, alleging the company encouraged Apple employees to leak information about upcoming products. That legal tension is worth monitoring.

Alphabet was down one point three one percent on the session. The company announced a collaboration with INVIDI Technologies to integrate Google Ad Manager for addressable advertising on unconnected set-top boxes globally — a programmatic expansion into previously unreachable households. It is a modest near-term catalyst but signals continued advertising infrastructure buildout.

Micron fell four point three two percent on the session. The afternoon rotation out of chip stocks hit Micron directly. Analysts cited in Tuesday's coverage argued the memory boom may last longer than consensus expects — flagging Sandisk's collapse as a potential buy-the-dip signal for the broader memory space. Micron's next earnings print remains the key fundamental catalyst.

Microsoft was up one point five three percent on the session — the strongest single-name performer in the portfolio Tuesday. Analyst coverage flagged Microsoft's July twenty-ninth earnings date as a potential inflection point, with one piece specifically describing the stock as positioned for a parabolic move post-earnings. That date is now firmly on the radar.

Amazon was up zero point eight zero percent on the session. No specific news drove the move. The name held up relatively well against the sector-wide pressure, consistent with its consumer and cloud diversification providing a buffer on down-tape days in semiconductors.

Turning to Advanced Micro Devices — down four point two one percent on the session. The chip rotation hit Advanced Micro Devices hard alongside its semiconductor peers. No company-specific news drove the move. The ASML earnings report Wednesday is the shared sector catalyst to monitor.

Tesla was down three point one nine percent on the session. No company-specific news was associated with Tuesday's decline. Tesla has been subject to broader risk-off pressure and remains sensitive to macro sentiment shifts. No near-term catalyst has been identified.

Over at Intel — down six point one two percent on the session. That is the steepest single-name decline among the primary holdings Tuesday. No Intel-specific news was cited, but Intel carries the most direct exposure to U.S. export control risk given its foundry ambitions and competitive positioning. The ASML report Wednesday is critical context.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, ASML quarterly earnings Wednesday — guidance on EUV capacity and China sales will move the entire semiconductor complex. Second, Microsoft earnings on July twenty-ninth — analyst coverage flagged it explicitly as a near-term inflection point. Third, monitor Sandisk's post-collapse trajectory — analysts are calling it a dip-buy in memory, which has direct read-through for Micron.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. ASML's earnings Wednesday morning will set the tone for whether Tuesday's semiconductor selloff deepens or finds its floor.
